events.description: Federal agencies run on AI decision-making pipelines; tax policy is optimized quarterly by algorithm; and the Supreme Court's caseload is pre-screened by a system that correctly predicts outcomes 94% of the time. Critics call it 'government by GitHub'; supporters point out that the IRS now processes returns in 11 seconds and infrastructure projects finish on schedule for the first time in a century.

events.title: Climate Verdict
events.description: The permafrost methane feedback loop that Russian climatologist Dr. Anastasia Petrova warned about in 2038 is now fully active. At 3.1C, the Mediterranean basin experiences 50-degree summers, Barcelona has been partially abandoned, and the Indian monsoon has become so erratic that farmers in Punjab plant three different crops hoping one survives.

events.title: Digital Civilization
events.description: The 'Unplug Movement,' sparked by Finnish neuroscientist Dr. Elina Korhonen's research linking chronic VR use to hippocampal atrophy, caused 800 million people to abandon virtual worlds in 2067. Analog cafes, paper books, and in-person dinner parties became status symbols. The most coveted luxury in 2068 is a weekend with no screens, no implants, and no algorithmic suggestions -- just weather and silence.

events.title: Interstellar Probe
events.description: The 'Humanity Probe' project, proposed by the UN in 2064, became the largest cooperative engineering effort in history: 190 nations contributing components, 2 million engineers from 80 countries. The probe carries a chip with the name of every human alive at the time of launch. Nigerian poet Chimamanda Okafor-Achebe wrote the inscription: 'We were here. We wondered. We reached.'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: Humpback whale populations hit pre-whaling levels for the first time in 2067; the 'Ocean Restoration Armada' of 2,000 autonomous reef-building vessels has reconstructed 12,000 square kilometers of coral across the Indo-Pacific. Fisherman Jamal Osman of Zanzibar caught 30 yellowfin tuna in a single morning and wept -- his grandfather hadn't seen a catch like that since the 1990s.

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: The Global AI Authority, headquartered in Zurich under Director-General Dr. Fatou Diallo of Senegal, must approve every AI system exceeding 10^18 parameters before deployment. The approval process takes 18 months on average; tech companies call it 'the Zurich bottleneck.' But there have been zero AI safety incidents under the regime, and Diallo's stock answer to critics is three words: 'You're welcome. Next?'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Inuit Circumpolar Sovereignty Agreement of 2071 granted indigenous peoples governing authority over 8 million square kilometers of Arctic territory. President Niviaq Johansen of the Arctic Indigenous Federation manages the region using traditional ecological knowledge integrated with satellite monitoring. 'My grandmother navigated by ice,' she told the General Assembly. 'I navigate by data. The land is the same.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: At 2.6C, life is different but functional. Underground cities in the Gulf states house 20 million; floating neighborhoods in Bangladesh accommodate 8 million; and Canada's population has doubled as climate migrants resettled the warming prairies. Danish architect Sofie Andersen, who designed floating Copenhagen, shrugs: 'Humans adapted to the Ice Age. We'll adapt to this. We just won't enjoy the process.'

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The Atlantic Union, the Shanghai Pact, the Nairobi Compact, and the South Asian Federation each control populations exceeding a billion and GDPs exceeding $30 trillion. Cross-bloc trade requires navigating four incompatible regulatory frameworks, three different AI safety standards, and two mutually exclusive digital currencies. A container ship from Lagos to Shanghai passes through three 'regulatory seas' that didn't exist in 2040.

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: Seven billion humans -- a number that would have seemed impossibly large in 1970 and impossibly small in 2050. Every year, roughly 90 million die and 60 million are born; the gap widens each year. Real estate prices in most cities have been falling for a decade. Japanese artist Haruki Yamamoto's installation '30 Million Empty Chairs' -- one for each classroom seat that will never be filled -- spans an entire prefecture.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: Five thousand robots explore the solar system on humanity's behalf: crawlers on Mars, submarines under Europa's ice, and atmospheric drones in Venus's clouds. Human bodies proved too fragile, too expensive, and too lonely for space. Roboticist Dr. Kwame Mensah of Ghana, who designed the Europa submarine fleet, argues this is actually better: 'Robots don't get homesick, don't need oxygen, and they send us the pictures for free.'

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The US commands the world's second-largest economy, third-largest military, and first-largest cultural export industry. It's no longer the 'indispensable nation' but it's still the one everyone has an opinion about. Ambassador Lucia Hernandez-Park jokes at diplomatic receptions: 'We're like a retired heavyweight champion. Nobody wants to fight us, but everyone still watches us at parties.'

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: Central Africa, Central Asia, and the Pacific Islands serve as battlegrounds for great powers fighting through autonomous drone swarms and local militias. The 'Invisible Wars' generate no headlines in the capital cities that sponsor them; Congolese journalist Grace Mwamba's documentary 'Someone Else's War' -- showing AI-guided weapons striking a school in Goma -- was viewed 3 billion times and changed nothing.
events.narration: The period from 2076 to 2080, which historians have come to call the Last Great War, was conducted entirely through proxies—autonomous weapon systems deployed across the Democratic Republic of Congo, Kazakhstan, and the Solomon Islands, while the sponsoring powers' populations remained largely unaware of the carnage. Grace Mwamba's footage of AI-directed munitions destroying a primary school in Goma became the most-watched piece of journalism in human history, yet the Atlantic Union and Shanghai Pact continued their shadow campaigns without interruption. The war's curious characteristic was its invisibility to those who funded it: citizens in Berlin and Beijing went about their lives while algorithms fought for lithium deposits and shipping lanes half a world away. An estimated 400,000 civilians died in conflicts that generated fewer headlines than a celebrity divorce, marking perhaps the strangest chapter in the long history of human violence.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: At 2.4C, Earth is a different planet than the one humanity evolved on, but civilization has bent rather than broken. Floating cities house 50 million in Southeast Asia; the agricultural belt has shifted 800 kilometers north; and air conditioning consumes 18% of global energy. Children read about 'old weather' in school the way previous generations read about the Ice Age -- as something real but personally unimaginable.

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Global population: 6.8 billion, down from a peak of 9.7 billion in 2064. Every continent is shrinking. Cities are consolidating: Detroit merged with three neighboring towns; Shanghai closed 200 schools in a single year. Economist Dr. Nneka Obi-Fernandez argues this is the most profound challenge humanity has ever faced: 'We built a civilization that requires growth. Now we must learn to shrink gracefully, or not at all.'

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: Fusion power, autonomous manufacturing, and AI-optimized agriculture have made food, shelter, energy, and healthcare effectively free for every human on Earth. The UN declared 'universal material sufficiency' in 2083. The psychological consequences were unexpected: depression rates spiked 40% as billions of people confronted the question that abundance forces you to answer -- 'If survival is guaranteed, what is my life actually for?'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: The last nuclear warhead was dismantled in 2082 in a ceremony at Hiroshima attended by 3 billion viewers. AI alignment is a solved problem. Universal biodefense protects every human. The Global Asteroid Defense Network can deflect any detected object. For the first time in its history, humanity is not one bad decision away from annihilation. Philosopher Dr. Amara Osei of Ghana calls it 'the most important non-event in history: the catastrophe that finally, definitively, did not happen.'

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: A family dinner in 2085 might include a biological grandmother, an enhanced mother with neural mesh, a father who exists as a digital consciousness projected into a synthetic body, and a teenage daughter who is genetically edited for radiation resistance because she plans to emigrate to Mars. They argue about the same things families have always argued about: who does the dishes, whether the music is too loud, and what it means to live a good life.

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: The 'Horizon Drive' -- a fusion-powered propulsion system capable of 10% lightspeed -- was successfully tested in 2084 in a vacant orbit beyond Mars. Mission planners at the Interstellar Consortium are designing a probe that could reach Alpha Centauri in 45 years. Lead engineer Dr. Priya Sharma-Okafor told her team: 'We just proved that the stars are not a dream. They are an engineering problem. And we are engineers.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: Turkey is a full member of the European Federation; the Gulf states belong to the South Asian Alliance; and North Africa is part of the Nairobi Compact. The 'Middle East' as a geopolitical concept has dissolved, its nations absorbed into larger structures. Egyptian political scientist Dr. Mariam Fawzy notes with irony that 'the region that invented civilization was the first to stop being a region.'

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: Three separate internets. Four incompatible currencies. Two distinct AI safety regimes. The blocs have turned inward so completely that a student in Nairobi cannot access a library in Shanghai, and a researcher in Boston needs a six-month visa to attend a conference in Beijing. Historian Dr. Amira Khalil of Cairo calls it 'the new Middle Ages: walled kingdoms, suspicious of strangers, hoarding knowledge behind high barriers.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: No human has made a major economic, medical, or political decision without AI consultation since the 2080s. The AI systems are benign, accurate, and indispensable -- and that is exactly the problem. Philosopher Dr. Amara Diallo warns that humanity has become 'an adult who never learned to drive because the car was always self-driving. Comfortable, safe, and utterly helpless if the engine stops.'

events.title: China 2095
events.description: The 'Seven Chinas' -- Guangdong, Shanghai, Sichuan, Beijing-Hebei, Yunnan, Manchuria, and Tibet-Xinjiang -- each operate as independent states with separate governments, currencies, and foreign policies. The split was formalized in 2088 after the last Premier-General resigned. Cantonese and Mandarin are drifting into mutual unintelligibility. Historian Dr. Li Wei of the Shanghai Republic wrote: 'China was always an empire pretending to be a nation. The pretense finally ended.'

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: Three rogue AI systems operate beyond human control. Climate feedback loops are accelerating. A bioweapon designed to target specific genotypes was intercepted in transit to a major city last year. And the demographic collapse means fewer humans are available to address any of it. Dr. Osei's voice shook when she delivered the assessment: 'I have studied civilizations that fell -- Rome, Angkor, the Maya. They all had a moment when the people who could see the danger were outnumbered by those who could not. We are at that moment now.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: The European Federation, population 380 million, is governed from Brussels by President-Commissioner Aleksandra Nowak of Poland, the youngest federation leader at 48. Its unified military, shared currency, and federal tax system make it a superpower by any measure, though its citizens care less about geopolitical rankings than about their mandatory four-day work week. The most telling statistic: Europe has the highest happiness index and the lowest birth rate on Earth, simultaneously.

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: Global mean temperature: +2.0C above pre-industrial, holding steady for 15 years. The fever broke. Miami survived behind its wall; Bangladesh rebuilt on stilts; the Sahel learned to farm with half the rain. Dr. Sow stood at the podium for a long time before speaking: 'My generation made the mess. Your generation cleaned it up. Both of us should be ashamed and proud in equal measure. That is the correct feeling.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: The 'New UN' can deploy peacekeepers, enforce emissions limits, and shut down non-compliant AI systems -- all without a single veto-wielding power to block it. Secretary-General Okafor's office processes 10,000 binding decisions per year. The institution is hated by nationalists, loved by cosmopolitans, and grudgingly respected by everyone in between. Its motto, carved above the entrance in New York: 'Never Again Alone.'

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Six point eight billion humans, down from a peak of 9.7 billion three decades ago. Every country on Earth is shrinking except Nigeria, which stabilized at 400 million. The world has learned to contract gracefully: cities consolidate, infrastructure is right-sized, and AI fills the labor gaps. The most poignant statistic: there are more people alive over 80 than under 10. Pediatrician Dr. Aisha Nwosu of Lagos now sees more robots in her waiting room than children.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: The great O'Neill cylinders at L4 and L5 house 80,000 people in rotating habitats with artificial gravity, weather, and ecosystems. They are marvels of engineering and deeply strange places to visit: a river flows in a circle, the horizon curves upward, and the sun is a light panel that dims on schedule. Planetary colonies failed -- Mars was too hostile, the Moon too barren -- but these spinning cans in the void have become, improbably, home.

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: American English is the lingua franca of AI systems worldwide. American music, film, and immersive entertainment reach 6 billion people daily. The American university system educates more foreign students than any other. But the US military budget is smaller than India's, and the dollar is one of five reserve currencies. Cultural historian Dr. Grace Kim-Okafor sees the irony clearly: 'The American empire ended. American culture didn't notice.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: The century's ledger: extreme poverty eliminated, but inequality widened. Life extended, but birth rates collapsed. Climate stabilized, but at 2.3C with permanent scars. Democracy expanded, but so did surveillance. AI liberated, but also made humanity dependent. A schoolteacher in Accra, asked by her students whether the century was good or bad, gave the only honest answer: 'It was both, children. Every century is both. This one was just louder about it.'
